critic_raw:
This CNN for knee replacement recovery time prediction experiment has several serious methodological problems.

1. OUTCOME SUBSTITUTION: PAIN REDUCTION AT 30 DAYS IS NOT RECOVERY TIME. The task description claims to predict 'recovery time' but the primary outcome is 'pain reduction 30 days post-surgery.' Pain reduction at a single time point is not the same as recovery time (which typically measures return to function, time to discharge, or time to meet discharge criteria). The design justifies this substitution by claiming 'pain reduction is highly correlative with recovery speed' — but this correlation is asserted, not established. If pain at 30 days does not monotonically correspond to time-to-recovery, then the model is solving a different clinical problem than the one hypothesized.

2. PREPROCESSING FITTED ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING. The design states: 'all transformers are initially fitted on the entire dataset before being applied uniformly.' For MRI scan normalization, this means the Hounsfield unit or intensity statistics that anchor the CNN's feature space include information from the test set's MRI acquisitions. Since MRI protocol parameters (field strength, contrast dosing, reconstruction parameters) affect image intensity statistics and these may correlate with patient outcomes, this leakage can introduce systematic bias in both directions.

3. CNN AND XGBOOST RECEIVE FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT FEATURE SETS — THE COMPARISON CANNOT ISOLATE IMAGE VALUE. The CNN receives MRI scans plus demographic/clinical/surgical features. XGBoost receives only 'features excluding MRI images.' If the CNN outperforms XGBoost, it is because (a) MRI images contain prognostic information, or (b) the CNN's multi-scale feature learning is better than XGBoost even on the shared tabular features. If the CNN underperforms, it is unclear whether imaging does not add value or whether the ResNet-18 fine-tuning was insufficient. A XGBoost model trained on the full feature set (including CNN-extracted image features) would be needed to isolate the value of image modality.

4. MAPE IS AN INAPPROPRIATE PRIMARY METRIC FOR RECOVERY TIME PREDICTIONS. Mean Absolute Percentage Error penalizes errors relative to the true value: a 1-day error on a 2-day true recovery is 50% error, while a 1-day error on a 60-day true recovery is 1.7% error. If the outcome distribution is right-skewed (short recovery times are common, long times are rare), MAPE will be dominated by prediction errors on short-recovery patients. Patients with complications and extended recovery — the clinically high-stakes group — will have low MAPE contribution and will not drive model selection. RMSE or clinical threshold metrics (proportion within 3 days of true value) would be more appropriate.

5. SAMPLE SIZE AND COHORT RESTRICTION CREATE GENERALIZABILITY PROBLEMS. The cohort is restricted to patients aged ≥60 over 24 months. With typical knee replacement volumes and the described data completeness requirements, the actual sample size is unspecified. Pre-trained ResNet-18 fine-tuning on a small orthopedic cohort is subject to catastrophic forgetting and overfitting. The design does not specify training set size, preventing any assessment of whether fine-tuning is viable.
all_issues_raised: Outcome substitution: pain reduction at 30 days is not the same as recovery time; the claim that they are 'highly correlative' is asserted but not demonstrated
MRI preprocessing statistics fitted on entire dataset before splitting leaks test-set acquisition parameters into training normalization
CNN receives MRI plus tabular features while XGBoost receives tabular only; performance gap cannot distinguish image value from architecture effects
MAPE penalizes errors on short recovery times disproportionately, systematically underweighting clinically high-stakes patients with extended recoveries
Sample size for ResNet-18 fine-tuning on an unspecified orthopedic cohort is not specified; overfitting risk and viability of fine-tuning cannot be assessed
Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting leaks test-period statistics into training normalization
Outcome (30-day pain reduction) is a poor proxy for the stated construct of recovery time
Surgeon ID as a feature encodes non-generalizable surgeon-specific patient selection and skill characteristics
Age restriction to 60+ creates a truncated-range prediction problem with limited external validity
MAPE is an unstable primary metric when true outcome values can be near zero
Outcome proxy (30-day pain reduction) does not directly measure the stated hypothesis target (recovery time); correlation assumption is unverified
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting contaminates MRI intensity normalization with test-set scanner statistics
CNN receives MRI plus structured features while XGBoost receives structured features only, confounding feature set inclusion with architecture comparison
MAPE as primary metric is undefined or explosive when true outcome values are near zero, which is common in pain reduction data
